text:
DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

Patient Name: Gerald Hutchinson
Medical Record Number: [REDACTED]
Accession Number: SP-[REDACTED]
Attending Pathologist: [REDACTED]
Clinical Service: Genitourinary Oncology
Referring Physician: [REDACTED]

---

SPECIMEN SUBMITTED
A: CT-guided core needle biopsy, liver segment VII lesion (3 cores submitted in formalin)

CLINICAL HISTORY
72-year-old male with known history of high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, diagnosed at age 67, cT3bN1M0) treated with androgen deprivation therapy, abiraterone acetate, definitive pelvic IMRT, followed by enzalutamide for castration-resistant progression, then olaparib for BRCA2-mutated mCRPC. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT) confirmed. Patient on olaparib for approximately 6 months with initial PSA decline; recent cross-sectional imaging revealed a new 1.4 cm hypoenhancing lesion in hepatic segment VII, indeterminate for metastasis. CT-guided biopsy performed for tissue characterization.

---

GROSS PATHOLOGY DESCRIPTION
Received in formalin, labeled with patient name and "liver segment VII," are three core needle biopsy fragments ranging from 0.8 cm to 1.3 cm in length and 0.1 cm in diameter. Cores are tan-white to pale yellow-tan, firm. All three cores are submitted entirely in one cassette (A1) for histologic processing. Estimated total tissue: 0.8 cm aggregate length of diagnostic tissue. Representative sections submitted for H&E and ancillary studies.

---

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

SPECIMEN A: CT-guided core needle biopsy, liver segment VII

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:
Sections from all three cores demonstrate infiltration of the hepatic parenchyma by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor cells are arranged in poorly cohesive nests, small acinar structures, and occasional single-file cords within a desmoplastic stroma. Individual tumor cells are polygonal with moderate to abundant pale eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclei are enlarged, pleomorphic, and hyperchromatic with conspicuous nucleoli. Mitotic figures are readily identified (estimated 3-4/10 HPF). There is no glandular luminal differentiation in the majority of the specimen; the few acinar structures present show compressed irregular lumina without mucinous content.

The surrounding hepatic parenchyma shows mild steatosis and portal tract expansion with mild chronic inflammation, consistent with reactive changes. No bile duct proliferation or significant fibrosis noted in uninvolved liver parenchyma. No normal prostate tissue is present.

The morphology is most consistent with a high-grade carcinoma with features favoring metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma, given the clinical history. The tumor does NOT demonstrate features of small cell or neuroendocrine carcinoma (no nuclear molding, no salt-and-pepper chromatin, no geographic necrosis, no Azzopardi effect). No squamous differentiation identified.

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMICAL STUDIES (Performed on Specimen A):

PSA (polyclonal): POSITIVE -- weak to moderate, focal cytoplasmic staining in approximately 60% of tumor cells
PSMA (GCP-II clone): POSITIVE -- strong, diffuse membranous and cytoplasmic staining
NKX3.1 (nuclear transcription factor): POSITIVE -- strong nuclear staining in >80% of tumor cells
AMACR (P504S): POSITIVE -- granular cytoplasmic staining
AR (androgen receptor): POSITIVE -- moderate nuclear staining in ~70% of tumor cells
CK7: NEGATIVE
CK20: NEGATIVE
TTF-1 (clone 8G7G3/1): NEGATIVE
CDX2: NEGATIVE
GATA3: NEGATIVE
HepPar-1: NEGATIVE (tumor cells; positive internal control in benign hepatocytes)
Glypican-3: NEGATIVE
Arginase-1: NEGATIVE (confirms non-hepatocellular origin)
Synaptophysin: NEGATIVE
Chromogranin A: NEGATIVE
INSM1: NEGATIVE
CD56: NEGATIVE
SOX2: NEGATIVE

Ki-67 proliferative index: Approximately 35% of tumor cell nuclei are positive (high).

Note: The weak/focal PSA staining is consistent with loss of PSA expression in high-grade or treatment-exposed prostatic adenocarcinoma, as is well described in the literature. The preserved strong PSMA and NKX3.1 expression confirms prostatic origin notwithstanding the attenuated PSA immunoreactivity.

---

ANCILLARY STUDIES

IHC Panel Interpretation:
The immunophenotype (PSA+, PSMA+, NKX3.1+, AMACR+, AR+, CK7-, CK20-, TTF-1-, CDX2-, GATA3-, HepPar-1-, Arginase-1-, synaptophysin-, chromogranin A-, INSM1-) is consistent with metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma. Neuroendocrine markers are negative, arguing against neuroendocrine transformation (treatment-emergent small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer). Primary hepatocellular carcinoma and adenocarcinomas of pulmonary, colorectal, breast, urothelial, or upper GI origin are excluded by this panel.

No additional molecular studies performed on this specimen at this time (somatic NGS previously performed on primary site re-biopsy specimen; results on file).

---

FINAL DIAGNOSIS

A. CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY, LIVER SEGMENT VII:
   -- METASTATIC PROSTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA
   -- HIGH-GRADE ADENOCARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH PRIMARY GLEASON GRADE GROUP 5
   -- NO NEUROENDOCRINE DIFFERENTIATION IDENTIFIED
   -- NO TREATMENT-EMERGENT SMALL CELL CARCINOMA
   -- SEE COMMENT

COMMENT:
This hepatic core biopsy demonstrates infiltration by high-grade carcinoma with an immunophenotype diagnostic of metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma. The tumor retains expression of PSMA, NKX3.1, and AMACR, confirming prostatic lineage. PSA expression is reduced (weak, focal), a finding consistent with disease progression and/or prior exposure to androgen signaling inhibitors (abiraterone, enzalutamide), which are known to suppress PSA protein expression independently of androgen receptor activity.

Importantly, neuroendocrine differentiation is absent by morphology and a comprehensive IHC panel (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, INSM1, CD56 all negative). Treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC), which can arise in the setting of prolonged androgen pathway suppression, is not identified in this specimen. Correlation with clinical and imaging findings is recommended.

The Ki-67 proliferative index of approximately 35% is elevated and consistent with the high-grade nature of the primary tumor (Gleason Grade Group 5, Gleason 9).

Androgen receptor expression is maintained in the majority of tumor cells (AR positive, ~70%), which may have implications for ongoing AR-directed therapeutic strategies in this case.

Correlation with prior tissue diagnosis, NGS results, and clinical context is recommended for therapeutic decision-making.

---

Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine
Report status: FINAL
